definition: The 50% midpoint (0.5 Fibonacci level) of a defined price range, calculated as (High + Low) / 2. It divides the range into premium (upper half) and discount (lower half). During consolidation or Seek and Destroy conditions, price gravitates toward equilibrium as a primary internal liquidity magnet when retracing back into a range after deviating outside it. Each new sub-range formed by a swing creates its own equilibrium level.
identification_rules: Identify a defined price range by selecting a swing high and swing low (or high/low of a candle, session, or wick extremes).
Calculate the midpoint: (High + Low) / 2, or plot the 0.5 Fibonacci level between the range high and low; this price level is equilibrium.
Mark the resulting level as a horizontal line on the chart; confirm range boundaries are clearly defined before applying, as an ambiguous range produces an unreliable midpoint.
Equilibrium above is premium zone, below is discount zone.
For expansion/continuation trades, price must hold above equilibrium on bullish retracements and below on bearish retracements; a decisive close beyond equilibrium invalidates the continuation bias.
When a range expands (new swing high/low), redraw equilibrium to the new extremes to keep it current.
In Seek and Destroy conditions, recalculate and mark equilibrium for each new sub-range (e.g., each new leg up or leg down) as price oscillates.
For reversal wicks, equilibrium is the 50% level from the candle body extreme to the wick extreme; price must remain on the reversal side of that level for the reversal to proceed.
In Candle Three Closure model, mark the 50% midpoint of the third candle's full range (high to low).
Align entries with PD arrays (order blocks, fair value gaps) in the correct half of the range relative to bias (discount for buys, premium for sells).
Multiple equilibria may exist across nested time frames and ranges; overlay higher time frame equilibrium levels to confirm directional alignment.
trading_implication: Equilibrium defines the premium/discount boundary, guiding directional bias and entry zones. It serves as the primary first take-profit target when fading a move outside a range (not a breakout target). For continuation setups, retracements holding above (bullish) or below (bearish) equilibrium confirm expansion. Entries at equilibrium yield 1:1 risk-to-reward; deeper discount/premium entries improve risk-to-reward. Used across time frames, it filters trades and provides invalidation levels.
common_mistakes: Attempting to trade breakouts out of the range instead of fading back to equilibrium during Seek and Destroy conditions.
Failing to update the equilibrium level as new sub-ranges form — after a new swing high or low is made, a new equilibrium for that leg must be recalculated.
Treating equilibrium as a reversal signal or standalone entry trigger rather than a reversion magnet and take-profit target.
Expecting deep retracements during expansion phases, when price usually holds above (bullish) or below (bearish) equilibrium on shallow pullbacks.
Searching for entry setups in the wrong half of the range relative to bias (e.g., longs in premium, shorts in discount).
Taking continuation entries after price has already closed or traded decisively through equilibrium, invalidating the setup.
Measuring equilibrium incorrectly, such as using candle bodies instead of wick extremes or neglecting to adjust for large wicks.
Treating equilibrium as a guaranteed reversal point or expecting precise touches, rather than allowing extremes to form before it.
Entering at equilibrium itself, yielding an unfavorable 1:1 risk-to-reward.
Ignoring higher time frame context and nested ranges, leading to confusion over which equilibrium to apply.
Forcing reversal wick trades after price closes through the wick's equilibrium, ignoring the shift in probability to the opposite extreme.
Neglecting to readjust the Fibonacci retracement as the impulsive wave extends, causing misplacement of the equilibrium level.
Assuming every touch of equilibrium will reverse without confirmation from lower time frame change of character or order flow.
merge_notes: 4 ICT + 1 SMC; picked ICT by majority. SMC card had no related_concepts; union includes all from ICT cards. Some identification rules from Education - ICT (Updated) and The Foundation were merged with similar rules from other cards. No major disagreements; all cards define equilibrium as 50% midpoint dividing premium/discount.
